title: Smoorsnoek
excerpt: Smoorsnoek is as Cape as you could wish to get. We grew up with the saying that you don't buy snoek in the months that don't have an "r" in them. So it's from May to August that you buy snoek. The reason is that the snoek is soft in …
html:
Smoorsnoek is as Cape as you could wish to get. We grew up with the saying that you don't buy snoek in the months that don't have an “r” in them. So it's from May to August that you buy snoek. The reason is that the snoek is soft in the months with an “r” in them. If you're making smoorsnoek, you can buy fresh or salted fish. If it's salted fish, just rinse it a few times well under running water. If you buy snoek from the boat, get them to fillet it and cut off the head for you right there on the quay. The fish doesn't have many scales, so it doesn't need to be scraped.

Last week I had the privilege of making smoorsnoek together with the lovely and dear chef William Baldie in kitchen.net's kitchen.

YOU WILL NEED:

  1. 1 snoek (medium size)
  2. 2 bay leaves
  3. 4 whole peppercorns (if you have them, otherwise leave them out)
  4. 500 ml water
  5. 2 onions
  6. 1 red pepper
  7. 1 green pepper
  8. 1 red or green chilli
  9. 4 potatoes
  10. 1 piece fresh ginger (2cm x 2cm)
  11. 2 cloves garlic
  12. lemon juice (to taste)
  13. salt and pepper to taste
  14. finely chopped parsley for garnish

NOW

  • put your snoek, water, peppercorns and bay leaves in a pot and cook until the fish is done (about 15 minutes). Let the fish cool and once it's cooled, flake it. Peel your potatoes and cut into dice-sized pieces. Cook them soft in the water you cooked the fish in.
  • cut your onions, green and red pepper into strips (remove the seeds) and fry in a little oil in a pan.
  • chop your chilli (remove the seeds) and ginger very finely and fry together with the onion and pepper mixture.
  • now add your fish and potatoes to the pan too. Season with salt, pepper and lemon juice – to taste.

And there's your smoorsnoek, done. I like to serve it on white rice with chopped parsley over the top for colour.

Sweet potatoes, tomato and onion salad (just with sugar, salt, pepper and brown vinegar) and bread with grape jam will round off the feast.

Sweet potatoes are easy to cook. Set your oven to 200 degrees Celsius. Wash the sweet potatoes clean. Rub them nicely with olive oil and coarse salt. Put them in the oven dish for about 30 minutes (it depends on how big your sweet potatoes are). Feel with a fork whether they're soft. The skin turns crispy. For extra deliciousness you can put a little knob of butter on your sweet potato when you split it open and serve.

And that's how we eat Cape style – snoek and sweet potato.
